speaker_turns.speaker_role: Senior Vice President & Chief Financial Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Good day to everyone and welcome to ROCKWOOL A/S Conference Call Regarding the Result for the First
Quarter of 2024. My name is Kim Andersen I'm the CFO of ROCKWOOL A/S. Today I'm pleased to present CEO,
Jens Birgersson.
For the first part of this call, all participants will be in a listen-only mode. As a reminder, this conference call is
being recorded. First, Jens Birgersson will go through our presentation and give an update on the results for the
first quarter 2024. After which, we will be ready to answer all your good questions.
Before I hand over the word to Jens Birgersson, I must ask you to notice slide number 2, which is the forward-
looking statement. Please be aware that this presentation contains uncertainties.
Now, we can go to next slide, which is Slide 3. Jens Birgersson, I'll hand over the word to you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Good day – with the microphone on this time. Good day, good morning everyone. Looking at the numbers for this
quarter, we started off in January with maybe the first two weeks, a little bit slow. You never know what happens
in January, but as the quarter progressed, business picked up and turned quite strong. There was quite a big mix
shift. We have very little residential business. We see very little activity still on the residential side, but on the
heavy densities, the non-residential [indiscernible] (00:01:40) flat roofs, sandwich panel, basically everything
going in the industrial segment, that started to grow and I'm very happy with our top line.
And considering that last year we kind of held back on resources, we have trimmed a bit of cost, but not too
much. When that growth came, that together with a bit of price level that was stable, that turned into a nice and
profitable quarter.
In terms of volume, due to the shift from the overweight on the heavy densities, we actually have a double-digit
volume growth in there compared to last year. Again, comparing with the relatively easy comparable.
Move to slide – take Side 4, please. Higher growth in the Insulation business. So we have 9%. Again, bearing in
mind that it was quite a hefty winter in some areas, especially Eastern Europe last year in Q1. On the Systems
business side, we see a decline. But that's picking up. We believe that the Systems will get back into growth
territory from now onwards. So it's looking better, especially Rockfon had a very slow start of the year, but it's
looking better now.
Go to Slide 5, regional sales development. Everything pretty slow in Western Europe. Should be said that
Germany had quite good growth and it has recovered a little bit, still on a low level but – and it's not double-digit
growth, but it started to grow and – but it was so extremely low for us last year. So – but that's the positive. All the
other countries are around 0%. No particular good news anywhere. But there is a solid business and possibly a
trend of improvement in some of the Western European market.
Eastern Europe then, there is a broad based growth, volume and top line. And to sum it up, I think there is only
one country where we didn't grow, all the others grow, and a lot of countries that grow-double digits. So that's
good. I think that fits with this and to try to understand why is that happening. It's heavy densities. It's industrial
buildings, it's non-residential segments. And I think it has to do with that people that invest in factories and those
type of buildings, they now see the end of the downturn and they have started to invest again.
Nearshoring possibly impact this and planning to be ready by the time it's turn – the business cycle is improving.
So that's what drives the business,
In North America and Asia we have a double-digit growth in both places. Japan where we made the acquisition, I
was there in Q1 is still a very small business, but we had the very significant growth and we are starting to get
some volumes into our factories. So that's nice. It's still a small business, but is encouraging the growth numbers
we see.
In Slide 6. Profitability, we had slightly lower prices compared to end of last year and just slightly below Q1 last
year we're talking. So, basically stable prices. We see logistics radius increase slightly. The mix impact our
profitability a little bit negative, but materials are lower and energy are lower. So, profitability is up plus the volume
growth, the big volume growth that also adds to the profit. And we didn't need to add much resources to produce
that growth. So, that goes through nicely to the bottom line.
In terms of provisions, you know, we declare provisions most of the time. We don't talk about it. But quarter last
year and quarter this year is roughly neutral. So, it's a margin improvement without impact of any provision taking
or restructuring or anything like that.
Slide 7, nice development in profitability on Insulation. You see, the margins has improved from a bit above 10%
to more than 15%. Nice. And that's driven by the business I mentioned. On the Systems division we also had the
– have the development last year, we were on 10.4% and now 14.2%, which is a more normal, normal margin. So
we're happy about that.
Q4 2023, I just want to remind you that we had the €60 million restructuring project that we booked in that quarter.
So that's not representative, but the Systems division has improved also, which is nice.
Slide 8, investment activities. We just keep investing. There are couple of things that will happen. One is that we
have started up the Flumroc plant. That was probably the main investment there. We also commissioned a new
paint line for Rockpanel and we keep doing that. So that has continued and we haven't had any – and nothing
especially notable in this. We just keep doing – executing on the plan and we haven't drawn back. We did also –
we see an increase, a couple of factors. Our technical Insulation business partly due to competitions exit from the
marine business, but also a general increase in technical Insulation demand. We have also invested in Bohumín
in an increased capacity and that business is growing nicely.
Moving on to Slide 9, the electrical melter in Flumroc. Okay, not everyone will know that, but it's in Switzerland
and it's in Flums. That's a €100-million investment. We made this acquisition and in the business plan. We made
it several years back. It's just been tremendously successful, but we made a commitment to secure this location in
Switzerland and this factory is in Switzerland, for Switzerland, which is quite unusual in our sector. We have now
completed that investment of €100 million.
It started up on the day it's the largest electrical melter in the world. And there are also another couple of extras in
that plant that we have never tried before that has worked out. And it has been a beautiful project. Co2 reductions
would be something like 75% with that, and it – we will have green energy driving that. So that's a really good
project.
It will also improve our ability to take back old products and do a circularity or upcycle them. We were almost at
the limit of what we could handle, but not a higher content of non-beryllium products. That's good. Just to give you
a little bit of feeling for this project, the total shutdown, I mean, we prepared everything and then we did the total
shutdown of five weeks. And to get through that, those weeks and supply the customers, you know, many
markets we can take in products from neighboring countries. In Switzerland, that is a little bit more difficult due to
the nature of the products they prefer in that country. We had built up a strategic stock of 100,000 pallets, and the
– it worked out really well. And we kept customer supplied.
Slide 10, free cash flow, good. As usual, net working capital is not a big issue for us. It's on a healthy level. Some
of the reduction is also caused by the increase in deliveries and that we now have relatively low stocks, but we
are ramping up capacities. So I'm not worried about being able to deliver good growth in Q2. We are ahead of the
curve and we should be able to grow more.
The share buyback program is progressing as planned and maybe we are – I will say we are surprised, but the
net cash – net, we don't have debt against the 200 – more than €200 million surplus again net debt free. So a
very healthy balance sheet position.
Over to the outlook, slide 12. We had already preannounced the outlook. We didn't change that now. So mid-
single-digit growth, 15% margin and we just maintained a – the CapEx forecast. So that's the outlook. And you
had factored that in already in your estimates, I think, when we did the profit warning a few weeks back.
Over to questions.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Danske Bank A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Thank you very much. I have two questions, please. The first goes to pricing. You've successfully more or less
maintained your pricing throughout 2023 and here in Q1, and while we see that competitors like Kingspan and
Saint-Gobain have been lowering pricing, can you talk a little bit to the pricing dynamics in the market and any
kind of feedback that you are getting from customers on this topic?
And secondly, with margins now at a higher level than we have seen historically, do you believe that there could
be a situation where this could trend even higher, if and when volumes bounce back to the level seen a year or
two ago? Thank you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Okay. Thank you, Casper. So let's start with the pricing. We did – first of all, the overall price number is a
combination, obviously, of all regions. And we see different things in different regions and we see different
developments in different segments. But underlying I would like to say, inflation is still there. Yes, energy prices
have come down to more normal levels and this and that. But we still have inflation in North America. We have it
in Europe and we have it in Asia. So going forward to keep profitability fine because the inflation has not
disappeared.
Net, obviously we have different pricing climate. So, in North America we have been quite careful on pricing until
now and the reason was that we got completely overwhelmed in orders and ended up with a very long delivery
time. So, now we have ramped up capacity and we have moved the – start to move through the backlog, get back
to proper delivery times. And we have also launched relatively – a substantial price increase in North America
again, because we don't feel we should raise prices and have loss of service to our customers.
So, US have a positive pricing climate with also high, high inflation and high demand. So, that goes that way. Asia
a little bit spotty, but again, in the big scheme of things, doesn't impact the overall numbers yet. In five years I'm
sure Asia will impact our numbers. But now it's not a big deal. And then we look at Europe and the tricky segment
would be the residential general building Insulation, where we really kind of walking a tightrope. But we are not
dropping a lot, but we did kind of adjust pricing a bit, but there is very competitive.
And then you have some markets where you have projects like mid-sized projects where we offer stone wool and
there is a complete carnage on the EPS side with very low pricing and we lose a bit of projects and we go down a
bit.
So -but overall on the heavy densities then, with the demand coming now and regaining ground then, we increase
prices. So, the net of that is that I see stable pricing, but it's a lot of work into different segment. You look at, for
example, technical insulation into oil and gas, into marine. A healthy, healthy demand and we pass on the cost
increases. So that's the way we approach it. And our goal was always to kind of get through here. And we have
always told our customers that we don't see the inflation go away. So we haven't – and it's not going away very
quickly. And therefore I see continued small price increases.
Then on the margins, Kim and I, we have this old 10%, about 10%, and we have never had the problem with
being, you know, 6% or 7% because when we look at the CapEx and the massive investments and the price of
the plants with the inflation, I would fundamentally say that we should have a higher margin in the business.
Again, higher margin is a bit of a dangerous concept because if we end up building, say, two jumbo lines and we
switched them on, it's several margin points in depreciation. So I think again, in future calls, I said that last time,
we should talk EBITDA margin, but I think we need a higher margin and we should try to maintain a higher margin
because with all these investments that are needed in this business, you have the – on the one side, the
greenification we do see or two take and I've mentioned €100 million in Switzerland, we need to charge for that.
You know, we cannot end up in businesses that don't cover them. So I think we need a – an underlying higher
profit margin. Then we also have now the Energy Performance of Buildings Directive that was decided on 12th of
April in the EU with very ambitious targets and I don't see an impact in 2024. But you have now goals for 2030.
You have goals for 2035, you have aspects in there on solar energy installations, which is a sweet spot for us
because of the non-combustibility. You even have some guidance in there on fire safety hazards, because when
you install more electrification, heat pumps, PVs, batteries, recharging infrastructure, you need to be more fire
safe. And then you have all these buildings that are going to be renovated.
So, with that, we also see that we – if that start to realize we need to invest more and therefore I will like margins
to be higher. Okay? So, nothing against maintaining the margins to make sure that we have a sustainable
business. I hope that answered your questions, Casper.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Danske Bank A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah, I suppose [indiscernible] (00:20:06) If I could sneak in one follow-up, it would be if there would be a margin
level where you would say, okay, now it's pretty comfortable, let's – let us try and go for more volume hunt instead
of further increasing the margin?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah, I think, we go for volume, but you know volume is super important. But if the volume gets restricted by
capacity, it's not smart to sell out your volume at too low margins, right? So, we need to build factories to cover
the volume. I mean, if – we definitely want to grow the volumes, but now we just come out of a period where it
was just to navigate maintaining market shares and keep profitability of the business. But we have been seeing
falling volumes. So now we come into growing volumes. So we have a different scenario, but for sure, for sure we
want the volumes to grow.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Danske Bank A/S
speaker_turns.text: Thanks a lot. Thanks a lot, Jens.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Kepler Cheuvreux SA (Belgium)
speaker_turns.text:
Hi. Hello. Good morning. Alexander from Kepler speaking here. Congratulations on the nice set of results. Yeah, I
was just wondering on the capacity, because you were already touching on that. In H2 2022, you did some
capacity reversal here, with unfortunate layoffs. And I think, one specific item you mentioned there was a deep
maintenance in the plant of Germany. Now that you mentioned that volumes are increasing and the demand in
Eastern Europe and Germany is increasing, have you already made some plans to increase capacity again?
Restaffing and – or are you comfortable with the current capacity? Thank you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah, yeah. I mean, the installed capacity is there and we are – like in the US, we had added several shifts. And
we are ramping up now in line with the capacity increase. And as I said, when we did that big – when was it?
2022. 2022 in the autumn, we did a big – a relatively big capacity take up. But that's a – so we struck early and
then we set the volume level for the downturn and that pretty much worked out. And also I assume you are – are
you in – based in Germany or no?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Kepler Cheuvreux SA (Belgium)
speaker_turns.text: Based near Brussels.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Oh, okay. A lot of [indiscernible] (00:22:53) and all the other schemes. Now we see, for example, is Southern
Germany in Nürnberg, with the growth that is – it's back from a low level. It's not healthy in Germany, it's nowhere
near where it should be. Nürnberg for example, in Southern Germany is back up and running again on all lines.
So we are putting in more capacity. But again, it's not – from the level we are talking about. It's not a big, big
problem. And we are planning that and we should be able to grow.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Kepler Cheuvreux SA (Belgium)
speaker_turns.text:
Okay. Thank you. And then going on on the capacity, my second question would be, if you could maybe give just
an update in terms of budget capacity and timing of the plants in France, Romania now also the US. Thank you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: I hand that over to Kim.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Senior Vice President & Chief Financial Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah, thank you very much. And we still have a plan to go ahead to construct the factories in France and
Romania to be online in the second half of 2026, crossed fingers. And then, of course the factory in Washington
State in the US has just been approved by the board here lately and we are in the planning there. We have a
piece of land we're doing the deals on that, but that will only come online in 2027. So that's the plan right now.
So, for us, the next new capacity will be most likely France and Romania here in Europe and in 2027 in
Washington State.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: And then India.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Senior Vice President & Chief Financial Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
And then India is also approved. And there will also be a three year period there. So maybe, yes, because it's a –
yes. Yeah. but it's a late 2026 into 2027 in India.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Kepler Cheuvreux SA (Belgium)
speaker_turns.text: Okay. Thank you very much, Jens and Kim. I hand it over to my colleagues. Bye.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Bank of America
speaker_turns.text:
Thank you very much. My first – I have two questions, please. My first question is on the margin. Is there any
reason why you are a bit less optimistic for the margin in the second half? Are you just being a bit cautious,
maybe on pricing? Or, is it possible that your 17% – 16%, 17% margin in the first quarter might be sustainable
into Q2 and H2? That's my first question.
And staying on the resi and non-resi diverging trends, clearly quite positive trends in non-residential demand.
Could you be a bit more specific about which countries and which verticals where you are seeing this strong
demand? Thank you very much.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Thanks, Arnaud. So, okay. Thanks. Thanks, Arnaud. So on the margin, when we did the outlook, we don't have
backlog. So we can – Kim and I what we do, we factor in what we see into the next quarter. And then we see how
the sales forecast build. But we don't have a long backlog and therefore we traditionally get this question often
when we do a forecast because we factor in what we see, and guess, you know, a couple of months out and for
the rest we take an assumption so that we don't come back and disappoint, don't deliver on it.
The Q – you know, the summer we have August, for example, where France is down and we have some other
things that the margin in that period would be a bit lower. Normally is. On the other hand, you have September
that normally is a super strong month. So sometimes that rates are there. But I will say the following rather than
trying – and I don't see that there would be a dramatic thing on pricing. I haven't factored in anything like that.
My intention is to keep pricing and keep working at under a price increases where we can and defend market
share where we should. And so, I'm not too worried about that. So I wouldn't put more into in other than to say
that we don't have a quantitative visibility into those quarters yet. We are getting deeper and deeper into it. But we
know, for example, in Q2, we know pretty much the business continues, it's growing, it's looking good.
Then we come on that – those segments and I would say, overall, the business is driven by the heavy business.
For example, solar PV is pretty broad based, is broad based. And then you have, for example, North America. It's
all types of business, residential, non-residential. And there we have, I would say, less of a – so for example, the
flat roof business in North America is not very big for us. So there you have a broad based uptick.
Eastern Europe is exactly what you would expect. You have manufacturing and logistics, data centers, all the type
battery factories, you name it, some electric vehicles factories, it's all those, nearshoring or just expansion in the
industrial base. That drives it. And that happens into all of Eastern Europe.
And then in Western Europe, I will say there is a bit of that coming, but I will say the fastest growing segment is
solar PV flat roofs.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Bank of America
speaker_turns.text: That's very helpful. Thank you very much.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, HSBC Securities & Capital Markets (India) Pvt Ltd.
speaker_turns.text:
Hi. Hi, Jens, and hello, Kim. I have two as well. So starting with the first one is on the – sorry I lost it. But I'll start
with the cost one then. On the cost side, what are you kind of seeing in terms of the dynamics? And if you could
just give us what you have hedged in pet coke in terms of pricing versus Q2 versus Q1?
And I guess previously you were talking about cash, you hedged for Q1 as well as electric – to an extent. So if
you can just give us a little more top data about where we are?
And then second question is on the margin, I guess many questions have already been asked about for it. But if I
recollect from history, you make a very good margin in your normal residential business versus flat roof. Given the
mix has shifted to flat roof in Q1 versus this – the historical numbers when the residential business picks up, could
you say that margin has kind of – has legs to go up as the mix improves positively?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: Thanks, Brijesh. Before I start, do you remember that question you asked about the CO2 intensity in the last call?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, HSBC Securities & Capital Markets (India) Pvt Ltd.
speaker_turns.text:
Right.
A
Jens Birgersson
President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
Yeah. I checked that a little bit. I just want to look back and comment because we didn't answer it when you asked
for it. So basically what happened is that we did improve the intensity. But what we had not done and that was an
omission, we should link that up. There were grid factors changing in Germany and France. Those were the main
contributors. So – and also China, where we did, we put in electrical melting, we did all the work, but then we had
not linked up direct. So those countries, especially France and Germany, had a worsening grid factor. And
mathematically that made CO2 look worse.
But when we go back and calculated it again and we match it with the racks, actually we did improve. So we will
not – that will not happen this year. It was a mistake from our side because we did not predict that Germany
would move so much to coal as it did.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Senior Vice President & Chief Financial Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
And it was a bit of an oversight with China, where we did do the investment in the conversion but didn't secure the
racks.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. So we did – so for example, China, we put in an electrical melter and then we did into the conversion to
green energy and that actually made it worse. But in effect we dropped the CO2 emissions with 70% or
something. So, that would be corrected. And then if you go back to your questions, you know, we haven't seen
residential come up, but I guess theoretically if you keep – if you keep the margin on the other business and on
the volume and there's more residential scope, the margin would go up and mix and then – so that's a point.
I will hand over to Kim soon on energy, but if you look – when I look at it generally we see a better material and
cost input side, but there are some categories is going in, up. And that's logistics, is radius, and there are certain
individual materials that creep up. But on average it's going down. Maybe Kim, you can throw some light on that.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Senior Vice President & Chief Financial Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
And I think we – I have told you before, yes. That we had this favorable deal on the coke for the first quarter. And
that for sure is one of the energy cost that is going up. I think it gas prices, electricity prices would be more stable
for the remaining part of the year. But pet coke prices for us at least compared to Q1 is going up. And then you
have also oil related prices like binders and plastic wrappings. Now are expected to go up in price. And then with
the insources and logistic and then in wages.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, HSBC Securities & Capital Markets (India) Pvt Ltd.
speaker_turns.text: Okay. Good fair enough. Thanks, Jens and Kim. Thanks for asking. Remembering answering that question.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: Okay. Thanks.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, JPMorgan Securities Plc
speaker_turns.text:
Good morning, Jens and Kim. Thank you for taking my question. Two for me, please. Just one on the strength of
the balance sheet, can you speak about kind of the intention in the mid-term between sort of CapEx growing the
business and buybacks? Do you think buybacks could be a norm going forward?
And then secondly, just to dive in a little bit on France, what was kind of driving the decline there? And do you see
that trend remaining the full year? Thank you very much.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
So, yeah, in France – I take France and then I hand over the balance sheet to Kim. So, in France we talked to a
very small, a single digit small decline. So I would say France has been quite resilient and done much better than
Germany. So I'm – I think there could be some of the schemes for energy renovation, there could be individual
project, but it's nothing dramatic. We are slightly, slightly down. So I would give it another quarter to see how the
quarter is coming out, out now.
And it – my prediction would be when France revs up, that we're going to have growth in France also coming. A
little bit like we saw in the Systems division, we had a gradual – a bit of a decline in Q1, but we see that coming
back to growth. So that would be my prediction. So I would just defer that one more quarter out and see if it was a
bit of a blip.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Senior Vice President & Chief Financial Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah, thank you, and Jens we also had [ph] had that in (00:35:28) France, we did the price increase in Q4. So I
think there was a little bit of pre-buying into. Anyway, on the balance sheet the board has decided this year to
propose this €160 million share buyback and they did that, obviously, looking at our own investment needs. And
it's not something they will do on a regular basis, but it's for sure. Now is the second time this – the board is doing
it in [ph] ocean hour time (00:35:57).
So it's definitely something that we will propose going forward in case that we can see there's excess cash in the
business to do this mixed of a continued sort of stable progressive dividend, then supplement that with a one-offs
and share buyback.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, JPMorgan Securities Plc
speaker_turns.text: Okay. Thanks, both.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Skandinaviska Enskilda Banken AB (Denmark)
speaker_turns.text:
Yes, thank you. Two questions from me. Firstly, just on the price action. So in the announcement when you
upgraded your guidance, you sort of reflect that the future inflation, you will take price actions. You've already
mentioned that you've raised prices in North America. So just want to check where else you have recently done
the price increases?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. Thanks, Kristian. So we have a – there are segments also in some countries, you know, if you take
technical insulation, sandwich panel, there we have raised prices. But if you – in addition to the countries you
mentioned in the Nordics and the UK, for example, we have launched price increases. And then we have been a
little bit careful in some of the markets. And so – but it depends a little bit how it develops now with volumes and
what happens with inflation.
But our – I'm not talking big price increases in Europe. We're talking inflationary, a couple of percent, 1% and that
– that's the outlook. But also segments where we need to defend the market share and therefore the net at this
stage, we don't factor in a price effect this year, basically.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Skandinaviska Enskilda Banken AB (Denmark)
speaker_turns.text:
Great. Understood. And then my second question goes to this investment in Flumroc, which you are highlighting.
So obviously a €100 million is quite a large investment. Can you speak a bit about the financial returns? So how
much can you then lift the predicted earnings in the Flumroc business as a consequence of this?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah, without going into numbers. So first of all, we – you know, to keep it to going concern and you look how
long we have run it without a lot of investments. You know you can – you need to look at it as an industrialist. And
it's a profitable business. And yes, we will improve margins. But the case we have for that is that we make
something like eight-year payback on that investment. And we are little bit more – you know, we accept a longer
period for a sustainability investment. And therefore, the eight years.
